next_steps: Inspect complete color photography and consult manuscript-specific scholarship.
Keep Furnivall's withdrawn Laud K 50 proposal excluded from the graph.
openQuestions.question: What exemplar supplied the eighteenth-century text on Egerton f. 271?

next_steps: Check wrappers, Chaucer Society records, and contemporary accession evidence; retain both dates until resolved.
openQuestions.question: How should the 1901-1902 issue statement and 21 February 1903 foreword colophon be reconciled?

next_steps: Compare other bound copies and issue records without projecting their contents into this carrier.
openQuestions.question: What carrier-state history explains the advertised but absent Gg Pardoner and Monk surfaces?

next_steps: Continue the British Library reproduction request and recheck item-level image availability.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public facsimiles of Egerton 2726 or Arundel 248 be verified?

next_steps: Submit a British Library photography request for complete color facsimiles with exact shelfmarks and work loci.
Assess the actual quote and written permission before caching or redistributing any supplied image.
openQuestions.question: What quote and written reuse terms will govern any future British Library photography?

next_steps: Retain both provider facts and ask U-M whether the repeated 00000710.tif event is intentional presentation behavior.
openQuestions.question: Why does the current U-M IIIF repeat one logical canvas while the raw package contains 786 unique TIFF files?

primaryTransmissionPaths.description: Direct principal transmission from the repository artifact through the U-M encoding and exact printed carrier to Furnivall's Dd.4.24 transcription wherever Dd survives.
primaryTransmissionPaths.label: Primary Dd.4.24 transmission path

primaryTransmissionPaths.description: Direct supplementary transmission from Egerton 2726 for Dd lacunae and the continuous ending after Canon's Yeoman VIII.855; exact source-switch notes govern boundaries.
primaryTransmissionPaths.label: Egerton 2726 supplementary transmission segment

primaryTransmissionPaths.description: Direct Clerk's Prologue and Tale segment through Wright's 1867 print, which Furnivall used for setting before recollating against Dd.4.24.
primaryTransmissionPaths.label: Clerk's Tale intermediary transmission segment

reviewNotes: Keep abstract work, manuscript witnesses, scholarly editions, print carrier, page images, encoding, preservation package, and repository XML distinct.
The U-M raw package is represented by a catalog_description schema adapter because the current entity enum lacks a digital-package type.
The two scoped version_of relations are schema adapters for proven Hooper visual adaptation only; neither relation asserts textual ancestry or facsimile identity.
The carrier includes twenty-three Ellesmere and seven Gg issued surfaces. Advertised Gg Pardoner and Monk surfaces are absent and remain a carrier-state question.
Furnivall explicitly withdrew the Laud K 50 hypothesis for the later Egerton f. 271 Parson ending; Laud is not modeled as a source.
U-M's 787 logical IIIF canvases and the raw package's 786 unique TIFF files are retained as different presentation and file-inventory facts.
Public-domain historical content does not transfer automatically to modern photography, provider metadata, OCR/TEI, or repository serialization.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a normalization-only descendant of the U-M/CME encoding embedded in a complete raw package. That encoding was produced from page images of Furnivall's 1901-1902 printed edition. The edition chiefly transcribes Dd.4.24 and fills its gaps and ending from Egerton 2726, while the Clerk text passes through Wright's 1867 print and is recollated to Dd. Tyrwhitt, Skeat, Arundel 248, Ellesmere, and Gg.4.27 have narrower editorial, textual, or visual roles.

catalogSummary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
catalogSummary.date: chiefly late 1380s-1400; this representation issued 1901-1902 with a foreword dated 1903
catalogSummary.title: The Canterbury Tales

catalogingSubject.label: The Canterbury Tales (Dd.4.24/Egerton 2726 Furnivall representation)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's unfinished Middle English pilgrimage frame and conventional twenty-four-tale collection as represented in Frederick J. Furnivall's two-part 1901-1902 Dd.4.24 edition, completed across Dd's lacunae and ending from Egerton MS 2726. The boundary includes prologues, links, Retraction, Skeat-derived omitted-line appendix, bilingual Angelus ad Virginem from Arundel MS 248, and exactly thirty issued Hooper woodcut surfaces (twenty-three Ellesmere-derived and seven Gg.4.27-derived). It excludes broad society matter and keeps abstract work, witness readings, printed edition, images, digital encoding, and repository artifact distinct.

contentParts.label: Group A, Fragment I
contentParts.summary: General Prologue; Knight, Miller, Reeve, and incomplete Cook sequences.

contentParts.label: Group B-alpha, Fragment II
contentParts.summary: Man of Law sequence.

contentParts.label: Group D, Fragment V
contentParts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence.

contentParts.label: Group E, Fragment VI
contentParts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence ending with the Merchant-Squire link.

contentParts.label: Group F, Fragment VII
contentParts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence.

contentParts.label: Group C, Fragment IV
contentParts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence.

contentParts.label: Group B-beta, Fragment III
contentParts.summary: Shipman through Nun's Priest sequence.

contentParts.label: Group G, Fragment VIII
contentParts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence.

contentParts.label: Group H, Fragment IX
contentParts.summary: Manciple sequence.

contentParts.label: Group I, Fragment X
contentParts.summary: Blank-Parson link, Parson's Tale, and Retraction.

contentParts.label: General Prologue
contentParts.summary: Pilgrimage frame, pilgrim portraits, and tale-telling agreement.

contentParts.label: The Knight's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse romance of Palamon, Arcite, and Emelye.

contentParts.label: Miller's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Miller interrupts the ordered tale sequence and introduces his fabliau.

contentParts.label: The Miller's Tale
contentParts.summary: Fabliau of Nicholas, Alison, John, and Absolon.

contentParts.label: Reeve's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Reeve answers the Miller and introduces his retaliatory tale.

contentParts.label: The Reeve's Tale
contentParts.summary: Retaliatory fabliau of two clerks and a miller.

contentParts.label: Cook's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Cook reacts to the Reeve's tale and introduces his own.

contentParts.label: The Cook's Tale
contentParts.summary: The deliberately unfinished fabliau of Perkyn Revel.

contentParts.label: Man of Law's Head-link and Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Host calls on the Man of Law, whose prologue precedes the Constance narrative.

contentParts.label: The Man of Law's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale of Constance.

contentParts.label: Wife of Bath's Preamble and Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Wife's autobiographical marriage argument.

contentParts.label: Friar-Summoner interruption
contentParts.summary: The Friar and Summoner interrupt before the Wife begins her tale.

contentParts.label: The Wife of Bath's Tale
contentParts.summary: Arthurian romance of marriage and sovereignty.

contentParts.label: Friar's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Friar introduces his attack on summoners.

contentParts.label: The Friar's Tale
contentParts.summary: Clerical satire of a corrupt summoner and a devil.

contentParts.label: Summoner's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Summoner answers the Friar and introduces his anti-fraternal tale.

contentParts.label: The Summoner's Tale
contentParts.summary: Anti-fraternal fabliau.

contentParts.label: Clerk's Head-link and Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Host calls on the Clerk, whose prologue introduces Griselda.

contentParts.label: The Clerk's Tale
contentParts.summary: Six-part verse tale of Griselda with Chaucer's envoy; typeset through Wright's 1867 print and recollated with Dd.4.24.

contentParts.label: Merchant's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Merchant responds to the Clerk's marriage tale.

contentParts.label: The Merchant's Tale
contentParts.summary: Marriage tale with fabliau elements.

contentParts.label: Merchant-Squire Link
contentParts.summary: The Host turns from the Merchant to the Squire.

contentParts.label: Squire's Head-link
contentParts.summary: The Squire begins his turn after the preceding Merchant-Squire link.

contentParts.label: The Squire's Tale
contentParts.summary: Incomplete romance of Cambyuskan and wondrous gifts.

contentParts.label: Squire-Franklin Link and Franklin's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Franklin praises the Squire and introduces his Breton lay.

contentParts.label: The Franklin's Tale
contentParts.summary: Breton lay of Dorigen, Arveragus, and Aurelius.

contentParts.label: The Physician's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale of Virginia.

contentParts.label: Physician-Pardoner Introduction
contentParts.summary: The Host responds to the Physician and calls on the Pardoner.

contentParts.label: Pardoner's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Pardoner exposes his preaching methods and avarice.

contentParts.label: The Pardoner's Tale
contentParts.summary: Exemplum of three rioters seeking Death.

contentParts.label: The Shipman's Tale
contentParts.summary: Fabliau of a merchant, wife, and monk.

contentParts.label: Host-Prioress Link
contentParts.summary: The Host calls on the Prioress after the Shipman's tale.

contentParts.label: Prioress's Prologue
contentParts.summary: Marian invocation preceding the Prioress's miracle narrative.

contentParts.label: The Prioress's Tale
contentParts.summary: Miracle-of-the-Virgin narrative.

contentParts.label: Prioress-Thopas Link
contentParts.summary: The Host turns from the Prioress to Chaucer the pilgrim.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Sir Thopas
contentParts.summary: Parodic romance interrupted by the Host.

contentParts.label: Thopas-Melibee Interruption and Introduction
contentParts.summary: The Host stops Sir Thopas and Chaucer introduces Melibee.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Melibee
contentParts.summary: Prose moral tale.

contentParts.label: Monk's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Host's response to Melibee and the Monk's introduction.

contentParts.label: The Monk's Tale
contentParts.summary: Series of tragedies.

contentParts.label: Monk-Nun's Priest Link
contentParts.summary: The Monk is interrupted and the Nun's Priest is called to tell a merry tale.

contentParts.label: The Nun's Priest's Tale
contentParts.summary: Beast fable of Chauntecleer and Pertelote.

contentParts.label: Nun's Priest's End-link
contentParts.summary: The Host responds to the Nun's Priest and transitions toward the following fragment.

contentParts.label: The Second Nun's Tale
contentParts.summary: Saint's life of Cecilia with proem.

contentParts.label: Canon's Yeoman Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Canon and his Yeoman overtake the pilgrims.

contentParts.label: Canon's Yeoman Preamble
contentParts.summary: The Yeoman describes his service before the alchemical tale.

contentParts.label: The Canon's Yeoman's Tale
contentParts.summary: Alchemy and fraud narrative.

contentParts.label: Manciple's Head-link and Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Manciple mocks the Cook and introduces his tale.

contentParts.label: The Manciple's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale of Apollo and the crow.

contentParts.label: Blank-Parson Link and Parson's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Host calls on the Parson for the final tale.

contentParts.label: The Parson's Tale
contentParts.summary: Prose penitential treatise; Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraphing.

contentParts.label: Chaucer's Retraction
contentParts.summary: Closing retraction after the Parson's Tale.

contentParts.label: Lines and parts of lines omitted by the manuscripts

locators.value: omitted lines and line-parts cited throughout Appendix 1

contentParts.summary: Editorial supplies from Skeat's 1894 edition, printed as Appendix 1 on pp. 679-686; this appendix is not a claim that Skeat supplied the main text.

contentParts.label: Angelus ad Virginem
contentParts.summary: Parallel Middle English and Latin hymn text from Arundel MS 248 f. 154, printed as Appendix 2 on pp. 687-688; the XML encodes the Middle English lines and marks the Latin column omitted.

contentParts.label: Twenty-three Ellesmere-derived teller woodcuts
contentParts.summary: The exact twenty-three Hooper teller-portrait surfaces issued in this carrier, copied and cut from the Ellesmere Chaucer; these are visual apparatus, not textual evidence for Dd.4.24.

contentParts.label: Seven issued Gg.4.27-derived woodcut surfaces
notes: Advertised but unissued-in-this-carrier Pardoner and Monk loci are Gg.4.27 ff. 306r/canvas 623 and 352r/canvas 715; their carrier-state history remains unresolved.
contentParts.summary: Four teller surfaces (Reeve, Cook, Wife of Bath, Manciple) and three paired allegory surfaces (Envy/Charity, Gluttony/Abstinence, Lechery/Chastity) issued in this carrier. The title advertises Pardoner and Monk blocks, but those surfaces are absent from the exact carrier and are not counted among the seven.

dateStatements.display: Chiefly late 1380s through the 1390s; unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame.

dateStatements.display: Issued in two parts, 1901-1902; foreword colophon dated 21 February 1903
notes: The range preserves the issued-versus-colophon conflict rather than silently redating the carrier.

extent.description: Twenty-four conventional Chaucerian tales are counted: twenty-two verse and two prose. The 654 LG and 17,450 L values are semantic XML element counts across the complete encoded representation, including prologues, links, and the English Angelus appendix; they are not critical stanza or line numbers. The 66-part human-readable inventory exposes conventional components and all four edition appendices, while the exhaustive structural evidence records 14 DIV1, 51 DIV2, 65 DIV3, 220 P, and 787 PB events referring to 786 unique TIFF names.

formStatements.description: Predominantly verse, with the prose Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale; appendices add parallel hymn text and visual material.

scope.description: General Prologue and multiple tale passages
Latin rubrics, phrases, and the parallel Angelus column; the XML marks the Latin hymn column omitted.

notes: Dd.4.24 is the principal witness, not the sole witness; Egerton 2726 supplies documented lacunae and the ending after Dd Canon's Yeoman VIII.855.
The current U-M presentation's repeated logical page event is preserved separately from the raw package's 786 unique TIFF files.
The exact carrier has thirty issued plate surfaces; the two additional Gg figures advertised by title are not silently counted as issued.

next_steps: Check Chaucer Society issue wrappers, annual reports, and contemporary accession records.
Retain issue and foreword dates as separate layer facts.
openQuestions.question: Why does Furnivall's foreword bear a 21 February 1903 date in a carrier bibliographically issued in 1901-1902?

next_steps: Inspect a complete color image of f. 271 and consult Egerton-focused scholarship.
Do not model Laud Misc. 600 (Laud K 50) as a source; Furnivall explicitly withdrew that proposal.
openQuestions.question: Which exemplar supplied the eighteenth-century Parson ending on Egerton f. 271?

next_steps: Compare other bound copies and Chaucer Society issue records without substituting their plate counts for this carrier.
openQuestions.question: Were the advertised Gg Pardoner and Monk blocks never bound into this copy, lost before scanning, or omitted from this issue state?

next_steps: Submit a British Library photography request for complete color facsimiles with exact shelfmarks and work loci.
Assess the actual quote and written reuse permission before caching or redistributing supplied images.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color facsimiles of Egerton 2726 and Arundel 248 be obtained under terms compatible with repository redistribution?

scope.description: Publication place of the Furnivall representation, not a claim about Chaucer's place of composition.
The ten Canterbury Tales fragment containers, including prologues, links, and Retraction; excludes all four edition appendices.
Dd.4.24 principal text, Egerton 2726 supplements, and the Clerk intermediary as separately modeled in lineage.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Latin author of Angelus ad Virginem
Anonymous Middle English translator of Angelus ad Virginem

scope.description: Middle English rendering printed in parallel

notes: The schema has no engraver role; adapter is used only for Hooper's explicitly credited visual copying and block cutting.

scope.description: Visual copying and wood engraving of exactly thirty issued design-bearing surfaces.

subjects.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: Pilgrims traveling toward Canterbury tell stories in many forms and social voices. This record describes the exact Furnivall representation chiefly based on Dd.4.24, with Egerton 2726 filling Dd's lacunae and ending, and keeps its narrow textual and visual appendices attached to their separate sources.

Carrier order: A/I, B-alpha/II, D/V, E/VI, F/VII, C/IV, B-beta/III, G/VIII, H/IX, I/X, followed by Skeat-derived omissions, Angelus ad Virginem, twenty-three Ellesmere-derived plate surfaces, and seven issued Gg.4.27-derived plate surfaces. The 130-node XML audit remains authoritative for the exact encoded hierarchy.
